exemplar.title: To what extent do the concentrations of NaCl and KCl affect the latent heat of fusion of ice, and how do the two salts (1g,2g,3g,4g,5g) differ in this effect?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent do the concentrations of NaCl and KCl affect the latent heat of fusion of ice, and how do the two salts (1g,2g,3g,4g,5g) differ in this effect?

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data tables and graphs are clearly headed with units and error bars, making processed results understandable.
Key equations (Q = IVt, L = IVt/m) are correctly presented and applied.
Graphical overlay of NaCl vs KCl trends with error bars facilitates direct comparison.
weaknesses: Uncertainty analysis is only applied to one data point; systematic errors (heat loss, instrument resolution) are not fully considered.
Units are inconsistently applied (J gâ»Â¹ vs J kgâ»Â¹) and some columns lack unit labels.
Quantitative trend analysis lacks metrics (e.g. slopes, RÂ²).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Communication and processing of data sit in the upper part of the 3â4 band, while consideration of uncertainties is in the lower part of that band. The work is clear and largely precise but has significant omissions in uncertainty treatment, warranting a solid score of 4.

strengths: Research question is framed within a specific and appropriate context, linking real-world observations to freezing-point depression theory.
Procedure, apparatus list, and variables are clearly presented, allowing reproduction with few ambiguities.
Controlled variables are described with rationale for their importance and methods of control.
weaknesses: Exact water volume and freezer temperature are omitted, hindering full reproducibility.
Heater placement and insulation details are not specified, creating methodological ambiguity.
Apparatus precision is inconsistently reported (Â±1 g vs Â±0.01 g).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands (methodology reproduction, methodological considerations) sit in the 3â4 band and one strand (research-question context) is in the 5â6 band. The overall level of detail, contextualization and variable control merits a high mid-band score of 5, reflecting minor gaps without fundamentally undermining reproducibility.

strengths: Conclusion restates observed trends and aligns with graphed data.
Qualitative comparison to established literature (Maus & DeSantis 2014) is relevant and shows awareness of scientific context.
weaknesses: No quantitative reference to gradient magnitudes or uncertainty bounds in the conclusion.
Comparison to literature lacks numerical alignment or discussion of discrepancies.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both strands fall in the 3â4 band: the conclusion is consistent with analysis but not fully quantified, and the scientific context comparison is relevant but superficial. A mid-band score of 4 reflects these strengths and limitations.

strengths: Identifies specific methodological weaknesses (heat loss, concentration range, measurement variance).
Proposes realistic and relevant improvements (better insulation, wider concentration range, additional salts).
weaknesses: Impact of each weakness on results is only superficially discussed without deeper analysis.
Suggested improvements are not always explicitly linked to the identified limitations.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evaluation strands both lie in the 3â4 band: weaknesses and improvements are described but not fully explained. A score of 4 rewards the specificity and relevance of the critique while acknowledging the limited depth of impact analysis.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student states the question clearly and embeds it within a **specific context**: an observed phenomenon on a frozen lake, the known use of NaCl for de-icing, and curiosity about the behaviour of KCl.  The background section explicitly links freezing-point depression, ionic interactions and latent heat, demonstrating why the chosen variables are worth studying.  This goes beyond a broad mention and provides a focused scientific rationale, satisfying the descriptor of a âspecific and appropriate contextâ.  Minor stylistic issues do not detract from the contextualisation.

Random uncertainties in time and mass are calculated for one concentration and propagated to latent heat; error bars appear on the graph.  However, the same treatment is **not extended to all data points**, systematic sources (heat loss, instrument resolution) are not considered, and explanations are minimal.  This shows âsome consideration with significant omissionsâ, fitting the 3 mark level within the 3-4 band.

The student lists many controlled variables, explains **why** each is important and **how** it is held constant.  While these points are mostly descriptive, a brief rationale is given for each, demonstrating an understanding of their relevance to data sufficiency.  Explanations of potential heat loss and salt-specific effects are present but not deeply analysed, so the strand meets the descriptor of âdescribedâ rather than fully âexplainedâ.

Methodological weaknesses (heat loss, concentration range, measurement variance) are **identified and described**; their likely influence on accuracy or validity is mentioned only superficially.  The impact is not analysed in depth, matching the middle band descriptor.

A numbered procedure, labelled diagrams and an apparatus list allow another investigator to **largely reproduce** the work; masses, heater power, thermometer precision and timing device are all given.  However, several ambiguities remain (exact water volume, freezer temperature, detailed insulation method, positioning of heater relative to ice), so replication would still require inference.  These minor gaps place the work in the middle band rather than the top.

The latent heat is calculated correctly from PÂ·t/m and averaged across trials; means are sensible and a trend is plotted.  Propagation is shown for one case and values are consistent.  Minor issues (use of J gâ»Â¹ instead of standard J kgâ»Â¹, rounding inconsistencies) constitute âsome inaccuraciesâ but overall processing is appropriate, earning the upper part of this band.

The student proposes realistic improvementsâbetter insulation, wider concentration range, additional salts, more precise setupâand briefly notes how they could enhance understanding or precision.  These are **relevant and described**, but explanations are cursory, so the strand aligns with the 3-4 band at 3 marks.

Data tables are clearly headed, include units and uncertainty notation, and processed values are presented alongside a graph with error bars.  Some precision is sacrificed (e.g. inconsistent decimal places, missing units in one processed table), yet the overall presentation is **clear and largely precise**, fulfilling the 3-4 band.

The student compares results qualitatively with Maus & DeSantis (2014) and cites freezing-point depression theory.  The comparison is **relevant but brief**; numerical alignment or deeper discussion of discrepancies is absent.  Hence the work meets âsome relevant comparisonâ rather than âjustified through comparisonâ.

The conclusion restates the trend (latent heat decreases with concentration; NaCl slightly more effective) and is **consistent with the graphed data**.  While generally justified, quantitative reference to gradients or uncertainty ranges is limited, preventing a fully âjustifiedâ rating, thus placing it in the 3-4 band.

annotations.comment: The research question is embedded within a specific and appropriate context, linking lake observations, de-icing practices and ion effects, demonstrating a focused scientific rationale.
The introduction effectively situates the research question in personal and curricular contexts, clearly motivating the study of NaCl vs KCl on latent heat.
The aim omits the specific volume of water used to prepare the ice; including this detail would improve reproducibility.
The theoretical background introduces $$Q=IVt$$ but lacks discussion of heat losses or assumptions, reducing methodological clarity.
Equation $$Q=IVt$$ is clearly presented with correct notation, enhancing precision in energy calculations.
The null hypothesis section cites a DSC study but does not align its rationale with the research question of latent heat change for KCl vs NaCl.
The variables section precisely defines the independent variable and its measurement range, supporting clear experimental control.
The controlled variables table is comprehensive but omits details like heater placement and exact insulation method, hindering full reproducibility.
The apparatus list specifies a digital balance with Â±1 g precision, inconsistent with the Â±0.01 g uncertainty claimed for mass measurements.
The procedure lists ice-cube preparation but does not specify freezer temperature or water volume, creating ambiguity in replication.
Raw data tables include uncertainty notation but the heater power column is missing units; adding âWâ would enhance clarity.
The calculation $$L=\frac{16\times1062}{51.12}=332.39\,J/g$$ is executed correctly but uses J/g rather than standard J/kg units; consider converting.
Uncertainty analysis is demonstrated only for the 1 g case; extending propagation to all concentrations would address significant omissions.
Table 8: Processed NaCl data is presented clearly with uncertainties for each concentration, facilitating precise interpretation.
Only the 1 g KCl case is processed; the absence of full processed data for other concentrations is a notable omission.
Figure 3 effectively overlays NaCl and KCl trends with error bars, aiding comparison of melting energy reduction.
The graphical analysis describes general trends but lacks quantitative details such as slopes or RÂ² values; include these for fuller support.
The comparison with Maus & DeSantis (2014) mentions qualitative agreement but omits numerical alignment or discussion of discrepancies.
The conclusion reiterates that NaCl lowers melting energy more than KCl but does not reference quantitative gradients or uncertainty bounds.
Evaluation identifies realistic improvements but provides only cursory descriptions of their impact on accuracy or precision.
The strengths/weaknesses table outlines valid points but should link each suggested improvement to specific methodological errors.
Further investigation suggestions are realistic but would be strengthened by explicitly linking each to one of the identified limitations.
The latent heat of fusion relation $$L=\frac{IVt}{m}$$ is clearly derived in Section 3, underpinning the data-processing approach.
The discussion of latent heat of fusion includes a correct historical reference to Joseph Black, enhancing scientific context.
In the raw data description, the fixed calorimeter mass is noted but its measurement uncertainty is not given; include it to refine uncertainty analysis.

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
